# `sesam verify --integrity` detects a sealed object that no longer matches the
# audit log (swapped or corrupted ciphertext on disk).

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Track a secret; it seals to an object under .sesam/objects.
exec sesam add s.txt --group dev
exists .sesam/objects/s.txt.sesam

# A pristine repo passes the integrity check.
exec sesam verify --integrity
stderr 'Integrity: ok'

# Corrupt the sealed object on disk.
cp garbage .sesam/objects/s.txt.sesam

# Integrity now fails. no-disk keeps the load from bailing on the same mismatch
# first, so the verify report itself is what flags it.
! exec sesam --verify-mode no-disk verify --integrity
stderr 'Integrity:.*error'

-- s.txt --
top secret
-- garbage --
not a valid sealed object
